query: How has sentiment toward the company changed over time and what events contributed to these changes?
prompt: Analyze sentiment evolution over time. Return JSON with: trend_direction, major_events, causes_of_change, business_implications, supporting_evidence.

pany s archives, this work offers a compelling narrative of Lufthansa s development set against the backdrop of Germany s turbulent twentieth century. Political, economic, and technological contexts are explored alongside the company s evolving brand identity, characterized by consistently innovative design. The book written by Hartmut Berghoff, Manfred Grieger und J rg Lesczenski provides insight into landmark aircraft, key developments in engineering and global reach, and the day-to-day operations that shaped the airline s direction. With rare photographs, vintage advertising material and internal documents, this volume combines historical insight with visual richness. ISBN: 978-3-7913-9388-9 (German)
ISBN: 978-3-7913-9389-6 (English) Reading sample The darkest chapter in Lufthansa's history was the period of National Socialism. Lufthansa becomes part of the Nazi regime as well as its war crimes and criminal activities.

retrieved_context.chunk_text:
\
\\
In 2026, Lufthansa will celebrate a significant anniversary: on January 6, 1926, 100 years ago, the first Luft Hansa was founded, and on April 6 of the same year, it made its maiden flight. \
\\
More captivating insights and exciting features about 100 years of Lufthansa can be found here: Lufthansa.com From pioneering spirit to modernity The development of German Aviation since the 1920s This richly illustrated volume presents the first comprehensive account of Lufthansa s one-hundred-year history from its pioneering beginnings in 1926 and its role during the National Socialist era to its postwar recovery and rise as a global player in aviation. Based on newly accessed sources and materials from the company s archives, this work offers a compelling narrative of Lufthansa s development set against the backdrop of Germany s turbulent twentieth century.
retrieved_context.title: History - Lufthansa Group

retrieved_context.chunk_text: s A380. In 2009 she was appointed Head of Major Tests at the Hamburg site, and from 2013 onwards she served as Head of Design, also at Airbus Hamburg. In 2014 Vittadini moved to Toulouse to assume new duties as Head of Corporate Audit and Forensic for the Airbus Group; in 2017 she was appointed Executive Vice President, Head of Engineering and a Member of the Executive Committee of Airbus Defense and Space; and in 2018 she was appointed Chief Technology Officer and a Member of the Airbus Executive Committee. Prior to her Airbus Group career, Grazia Vittadini served as a stress and design engineer at Fairchild Dornier in Oberpfaffenhofen from 2000 to 2002, and as a focal point for the Eurofighter Role Equipment Program at AEREA Aircraft Equipment of Milan from 1999 to 2000. She began her career in 1998, as Head of the Technical Department at Marves (Milan).

retrieved_context.chunk_text:
shaped Germany like no other company. Friedrich Merz
Chancellor of the Federal Republic of Germany In his address, then-Supervisory Board Chairman Dr. Karl-Ludwig Kley addressed current challenges to the industry s competitiveness. He criticized unilateral EU regulations that burden European airlines and called for corrections. Climate legislation, in particular, is making flying increasingly expensive in the EU and thereby weakening Europe s strategic autonomy. Address by Dr. Karl-Ludwig Kley, then-Chairman of the Supervisory Board of the Lufthansa Group Carsten Spohr, CEO of the Lufthansa Group, took guests on a journey through a century of corporate history. Lufthansa evolved from the former state airline into one of the world s leading airline groups. The recipe for success applies to both aviation and politics: Only a European team can hold its own against the U.S. and China. Speech by Carsten Spohr, CEO of the Lufthansa Group

retrieved_context.chunk_text:
omically challenging times. One example is the call to move quickly to regulate so-called non-CO2 climate effects in aviation, particularly contrails. The article from Science reproduced in this brief shows that the climate impact of contrails may be lower than previously assumed. Premature EU regulation on this basis would place a unilateral burden on European airlines, further distort competition, and bring no benefit to the climate. Further research should therefore precede any new regulation. We hope you find this an insightful read. Andreas Bartels
Head of Corporate Communications
Lufthansa Group Dr. Kay Lindemann
Head of Corporate International Relations
and Government Affairs
Lufthansa Group Middle East Aviation in crisis mode Far too high location costs, spiking jet fuel prices, costly labor disputes: the economic pressure on aviation in Germany is growing steadily.

retrieved_context.chunk_text: n an. Zur Absicherung gegen m gliche Krisen wird die Lufthansa Group weiterhin eine konservative Mindestliquidit t von 8-10 Milliarden Euro vorhalten. Dar ber hinaus wird das Unternehmen an der bestehenden Dividendenpolitik festhalten, welche eine Aussch ttung von 20 bis 40 Prozent des Konzerngewinns an die Aktion re vorsieht. Durch die integrierte Zusammenarbeit innerhalb der Lufthansa Group werden sich Prozesse und Strukturen in der Zusammenarbeit der Konzerngesellschaften in Zukunft stark ver ndern. Auf dieser Grundlage pr ft das Unternehmen, welche T tigkeiten zum Beispiel aufgrund von Doppelarbeiten in Zukunft nicht mehr wie bisher erforderlich sein werden. Insbesondere die tiefgreifenden Umw lzungen durch Digitalisierung und der vermehrte Einsatz von k nstlicher Intelligenz werden in vielen Bereichen und Prozessen f r mehr Effizienz sorgen.

retrieved_context.chunk_text: y for Lufthansa's regional partnerships in Europe in 1998. In 2000, Carsten Spohr was appointed head of the alliance management of Deutsche Lufthansa. In this function, he was responsible for the management and coordination of Lufthansa's worldwide cooperation partners, including the Star Alliance and regional partners. In 2003, he also assumed responsibility for the passenger strategy and the passenger shareholdings of Deutsche Lufthansa. In October 2004, Carsten Spohr was appointed to the Executive Board of Lufthansa Passenger Airlines. His responsibilities included the hub management, cabin crew and personnel management. Carsten Spohr was appointed Chairman of the Executive Board of Lufthansa Cargo AG with effect from 15 January 2007. From 1 January 2011, he was a member of the Executive Board of Deutsche Lufthansa AG and Chairman of the Lufthansa Passage Executive Board. Carsten Spohr holds the Lufthansa Captain's license for the Airbus A320 Family aircraft types.
retrieved_context.title: Carsten Spohr - Lufthansa Group

retrieved_context.chunk_text: company will adhere to its existing dividend policy, which provides for a distribution of 20 to 40 percent of consolidated net income to shareholders. Integrated cooperation within the Lufthansa Group will lead to significant changes in the processes and structures governing cooperation between Group companies in the future. On this basis, the Lufthansa Group is reviewing which activities will no longer be necessary in the future, for example due to duplication of work. In particular, the profound changes brought about by digitalization and the increased use of artificial intelligence will lead to greater efficiency in many areas and processes. Due to these developments and structural adjustments, the Lufthansa Group plans to cut a total of around 4,000 jobs worldwide by 2030, the majority of which will be in Germany. This will be done in consultation with the social partners. The focus will be on administrative rather than operational roles. More information you can find here:
retrieved_context.title: Lufthansa Group is consistently pursuing its strategy and aims to significantly increase profitability

llm_output:
Here's a thinking process:

1.  **Analyze User Input:**
   - **Task:** Analyze sentiment evolution over time.
   - **Output Format:** JSON with specific keys: `trend_direction`, `major_events`, `causes_of_change`, `business_implications`, `supporting_evidence`.
   - **Constraints:** 
     - Analyze ONLY the provided retrieved documents.
     - If evidence is unavailable, return an empty array.
     - Every finding must be supported by evidence from the retrieved documents.
     - Confidence score must be between 0 and 100.
     - Output must be directly usable by a dashboard frontend (valid JSON only).
     - Do NOT explain reasoning, include thinking steps, markdown, or text before/after JSON.
     - Return valid JSON only.

2.  **Scan Retrieved Context for Sentiment/Time Evolution:**
   - The documents are mostly about Lufthansa Group's history, corporate strategy, investor relations, and policy briefs from around 2026.
   - I need to extract information about sentiment evolution over time. Sentiment in a corporate/aviation context usually refers to public perception, market sentiment, strategic shifts, or historical narrative tone.
   - Let's look for chronological markers and sentiment indicators:
     - [1], [2], [3]: Discuss Lufthansa's 100th anniversary (1926-2026). Mentions "darkest chapter" during National Socialism, war crimes, criminal activities. Also mentions postwar recovery, rise as a global player, and current efforts to examine responsibility. Tone: Historical reflection, acknowledging past negativity, moving toward modernity and corporate responsibility.
     - [6], [10]: Investor relations data. Share price: 9.06 EUR, down 0.11 (-1.16%) on 06/19/2026. Mentions "Current Investor Presentation (June 2026)".
     - [7]: Policy Brief May 2026. Addresses current challenges: EU regulations burdening airlines, climate legislation making flying expensive, weakening strategic autonomy. CEO Carsten Spohr and Supervisory Board Chairman Karl-Ludwig Kley speak. Tone: Critical of unilateral EU regulations, emphasizing European competitiveness.
     - [11]: Policy Brief May 2026. Discusses non-CO2 climate effects (contrails), warns against premature EU regulation, cites economic pressure: high location costs, spiking jet fuel prices, costly labor disputes. Tone: Concerned about regulatory and economic pressures.
     - [12], [14]: Corporate strategy update (June 2026). Focus on profitability, maintaining 8-10B EUR liquidity, dividend policy (20-40%), digitalization/AI for efficiency, cutting ~4,000 jobs by 2030 (mostly admin, in Germany). Tone: Strategic, efficiency-driven, proactive but acknowledging structural changes.
     - [13]: Carsten Spohr's career timeline (1998-2011+). Shows internal progression.
     - [4]: Grazia Vittadini's career at Airbus (not directly Lufthansa sentiment, but shows industry context).
     - [5], [8], [9], [15]: Mostly UI/cookie/video player text. Irrelevant.

3.  **Synthesize Sentiment Evolution over Time:**
   - *Historical (1926-1945):* Negative/Dark. "Darkest chapter", "National Socialism", "war crimes and criminal activities".
   - *Post-War to Modern Era:* Positive/Resilient. "Postwar recovery and rise as a global player", "pioneering spirit to modernity", "consistently innovative design".
   - *Current (2026):* Cautious/Strategic/Defensive. Facing economic pressure (fuel, labor, location costs), regulatory burden (EU climate rules), but responding with efficiency drives (AI, digitalization, job cuts), maintaining liquidity/dividends, and advocating for European competitiveness. Sentiment is pragmatic, focused on survival/profitability amid external pressures.
   - *Market/Investor Sentiment (2026):* Slightly negative/neutral. Share price down 1.16% on June 19, 2026. Focus on profitability and structural adjustments.

4.  **Map to Required JSON Structure:**
   - `trend_direction`: Needs to describe the overall shift. From historical darkness to postwar recovery, to current strategic caution/efficiency focus amid regulatory/economic headwinds.
   - `major_events`: 100th anniversary (2026), historical
